title: Pad Thai with Prawns and Peanuts

ingredients.name: flat rice noodles, 3-5 mm width
visual_checks: soak in cold water 20 minutes until pliable, then drain
ingredients.notes: soak in cold water 20 minutes until pliable, then drain

subs.name: 200 g chicken strips or firm tofu for non-seafood versions
subs.notes: chicken needs 4 minutes; tofu should be pressed and pan-fried first

ingredients.name: spring onions, cut into 3 cm pieces
tamarind pulp, dissolved in 4 tbsp warm water and strained

subs.name: 2 tbsp lime juice plus 1 tsp brown sugar
2.5 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt
subs.notes: slightly less complex but works well

subs.name: roasted cashew pieces or toasted sesame seeds for peanut allergy

ingredients.name: lime or lemon wedges, to serve
dried red chilli flakes, to garnish

steps.text: Soak the rice noodles in cold water for 20 minutes until they are pliable and bend without snapping but are not fully soft — they will finish cooking in the wok. Drain and set aside. Meanwhile, mix the tamarind liquid, fish sauce and sugar in a small bowl to make the pad Thai sauce.
Heat a wok or large kadai on the highest flame your stove allows. Add 2 tablespoons of oil and, when it shimmers, add the minced garlic. Stir-fry for 20 seconds until lightly golden.

Add the prawns and stir-fry for 2 minutes until they turn pink and curl. Push them to the side of the wok, add the remaining tablespoon of oil to the centre, crack in the eggs and scramble them lightly. When the eggs are half-set, fold everything together.

Add the drained noodles to the wok and pour the pad Thai sauce over them immediately. Toss everything together on high heat for 2-3 minutes, using tongs or two spatulas, until the noodles absorb the sauce and pick up a little colour at the edges.
Add the bean sprouts and the white parts of the spring onion. Toss once or twice — 30 seconds maximum — to keep the sprouts crunchy. Taste and adjust fish sauce for salt or sugar for sweetness.

Plate immediately. Scatter crushed peanuts and the green parts of the spring onion over the top. Serve with a lime wedge for squeezing and a pinch of chilli flakes on the side for those who want more heat.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: prawn → 200 g chicken strips or firm tofu for non-seafood versions (chicken needs 4 minutes; tofu should be pressed and pan-fried first); fish-sauce → 2.5 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt (slightly less complex but works well); tamarind → 2 tbsp lime juice plus 1 tsp brown sugar (less deep but tangy); peanut → roasted cashew pieces or toasted sesame seeds for peanut allergy (cashews give a richer mouthfeel)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: High heat is the soul of Pad Thai — a cast-iron kadai on a gas burner works perfectly. Use your stove's largest burner at full power. Flat rice noodles (called 'rice noodles' or 'banh pho' in Indian stores) are sold in most large supermarkets and online. Tamarind pulp (imli) is at every Indian grocery store; dissolve a small ball the size of a cherry in warm water and strain. Fish sauce is available at select gourmet stores and on Amazon India; soy sauce is a straightforward substitute.
